APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): Yes — this is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses an NLP task (argumentation mining) and introduces a neural architecture (modified Pointer Network with joint AC-type and link prediction) evaluated against strong baselines on two standard corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). The methodology (biLSTM encoder/decoder, joint loss, hyperparameter reporting) and empirical claims of state-of-the-art results align with the methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues. While the paper notes limitations (small data, segmentation not addressed, AC representations not learned end-to-end), these are typical incremental follow-ups and do not undermine its relevance to the venue's audience. | B(4): The paper addresses a clearly NLP-centric problem (argumentation mining) and proposes a neural sequence-to-sequence/Pointer Network approach with a joint AC-type and link prediction objective, which is directly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. It demonstrates state-of-the-art results on two standard corpora (Persuasive Essays and Microtexts) and compares against appropriate baselines, using methods and evaluation practices expected at these conferences. Minor scope concerns are that segmentation and end-to-end AC representation learning are left for future work and the model assumes a tree structure, but these do not substantially undermine its relevance to the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes — this is squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses an NLP task (argumentation mining) and introduces a neural architecture (modified Pointer Network with joint AC-type and link prediction) evaluated against strong baselines on two standard corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). The methodology (biLSTM encoder/decoder, joint loss, hyperparameter reporting) and empirical claims of state-of-the-art results align with the methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues. While the paper notes limitations (small data, segmentation not addressed, AC representations not learned end-to-end), these are typical incremental follow-ups and do not undermine its relevance to the venue's audience.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a clearly NLP-centric problem (argumentation mining) and proposes a neural sequence-to-sequence/Pointer Network approach with a joint AC-type and link prediction objective, which is directly relevant to top NLP/ML venues. It demonstrates state-of-the-art results on two standard corpora (Persuasive Essays and Microtexts) and compares against appropriate baselines, using methods and evaluation practices expected at these conferences. Minor scope concerns are that segmentation and end-to-end AC representation learning are left for future work and the model assumes a tree structure, but these do not substantially undermine its relevance to the venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates the proposed joint Pointer Network on two standard corpora and compares against multiple strong baselines (including a regular Pointer Network and prior state-of-the-art), which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. However, the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., explicit studies of the joint loss weighting α or removal of the AC-type head), so some methodological contributions are not isolated. Related work is described as thorough and several limitations are acknowledged (high dropout due to small data, non-end-to-end AC representations, segmentation not addressed), and hyperparameters are reported, but evaluation metrics and code availability are not mentioned, modestly limiting reproducibility. | B(4): The paper provides solid experimental support: it evaluates the joint model against multiple baselines (including a regular Pointer Network) on two corpora and reports state-of-the-art results, and hyperparameters are reported. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations (need for high dropout, non-end-to-end AC representations, and segmentation not addressed) are explicitly discussed. However, ablation/sensitivity analysis appears limited (no explicit mention of systematic studies over the joint-loss weight α, dropout sensitivity, or other component-wise ablations), and key reproducibility aids are missing from the summary (evaluation metrics not specified and code availability not mentioned).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed joint Pointer Network on two standard corpora and compares against multiple strong baselines (including a regular Pointer Network and prior state-of-the-art), which provides solid empirical support for the main claims. However, the summary does not report ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., explicit studies of the joint loss weighting α or removal of the AC-type head), so some methodological contributions are not isolated. Related work is described as thorough and several limitations are acknowledged (high dropout due to small data, non-end-to-end AC representations, segmentation not addressed), and hyperparameters are reported, but evaluation metrics and code availability are not mentioned, modestly limiting reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid experimental support: it evaluates the joint model against multiple baselines (including a regular Pointer Network) on two corpora and reports state-of-the-art results, and hyperparameters are reported. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations (need for high dropout, non-end-to-end AC representations, and segmentation not addressed) are explicitly discussed. However, ablation/sensitivity analysis appears limited (no explicit mention of systematic studies over the joint-loss weight α, dropout sensitivity, or other component-wise ablations), and key reproducibility aids are missing from the summary (evaluation metrics not specified and code availability not mentioned).

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper applies and adapts Pointer Networks to the specific task of extracting links between argument components and introduces a joint model that simultaneously predicts AC types and links, which is a clear methodological novelty relative to the cited classical baselines and to a regular Pointer Network. The authors explicitly claim this is the first neural-network-based approach for this subtask and demonstrate state-of-the-art results on two corpora, differentiating their contribution from prior ILP/MST/SVM-based work. However, the core building blocks (Pointer Network, BiLSTM encoder/decoder, joint loss) are established techniques, so the contribution is a notable and well-differentiated adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. | B(4): The paper applies a modified Pointer Network and introduces a joint objective to predict both argument-component types and links simultaneously—an approach not used in prior (feature-based or graph-based) work and claimed as the first neural-network method for this subtask. The method is clearly differentiated from previous SVM/ILP and parser-based baselines and shows empirical gains (SOTA on two corpora), indicating a non-trivial extension of existing sequence-to-sequence/attention techniques to argumentation mining. While the underlying problem is well-studied, the specific architectural adaptation and joint training constitute notable novelty that would inform experts in the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies and adapts Pointer Networks to the specific task of extracting links between argument components and introduces a joint model that simultaneously predicts AC types and links, which is a clear methodological novelty relative to the cited classical baselines and to a regular Pointer Network. The authors explicitly claim this is the first neural-network-based approach for this subtask and demonstrate state-of-the-art results on two corpora, differentiating their contribution from prior ILP/MST/SVM-based work. However, the core building blocks (Pointer Network, BiLSTM encoder/decoder, joint loss) are established techniques, so the contribution is a notable and well-differentiated adaptation rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies a modified Pointer Network and introduces a joint objective to predict both argument-component types and links simultaneously—an approach not used in prior (feature-based or graph-based) work and claimed as the first neural-network method for this subtask. The method is clearly differentiated from previous SVM/ILP and parser-based baselines and shows empirical gains (SOTA on two corpora), indicating a non-trivial extension of existing sequence-to-sequence/attention techniques to argumentation mining. While the underlying problem is well-studied, the specific architectural adaptation and joint training constitute notable novelty that would inform experts in the field.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (Stab and Gurevych 2016’s Base Classifier and ILP Joint Model, several Peldszus & Stede 2015 systems, and a regular Pointer Network) and evaluates on the same two standard corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). Related work coverage is described as thorough and the proposed joint model is analyzed relative to a regular Pointer Network and traditional feature-rich systems. However the summary does not specify evaluation metrics or detailed experimental conditions, so some aspects of fairness and whether prior methods are acknowledged as performing comparably are unclear, resulting in a minor deduction. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Stab & Gurevych 2016 Base Classifier and ILP Joint Model; Peldszus & Stede 2015 variants; and a regular Pointer Network) on the same two corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the authors include an ablation (regular Pointer Network) and a joint-model comparison, indicating clear differences are tested. However, the summary does not state the evaluation metrics used and does not explicitly say whether prior methods are acknowledged when they perform comparably, leaving some uncertainty about full fairness and discussion of comparable results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to appropriate and recent baselines (Stab and Gurevych 2016’s Base Classifier and ILP Joint Model, several Peldszus & Stede 2015 systems, and a regular Pointer Network) and evaluates on the same two standard corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). Related work coverage is described as thorough and the proposed joint model is analyzed relative to a regular Pointer Network and traditional feature-rich systems. However the summary does not specify evaluation metrics or detailed experimental conditions, so some aspects of fairness and whether prior methods are acknowledged as performing comparably are unclear, resulting in a minor deduction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Stab & Gurevych 2016 Base Classifier and ILP Joint Model; Peldszus & Stede 2015 variants; and a regular Pointer Network) on the same two corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the authors include an ablation (regular Pointer Network) and a joint-model comparison, indicating clear differences are tested. However, the summary does not state the evaluation metrics used and does not explicitly say whether prior methods are acknowledged when they perform comparably, leaving some uncertainty about full fairness and discussion of comparable results.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—automatic extraction of argumentative links and component types—and introduces a novel joint pointer-network-based architecture that achieves state-of-the-art results on two corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). As the first neural approach focused on link extraction and AC type classification, it is likely to shift research toward neural, end-to-end methods and inspire follow-up work (e.g., learning AC representations, handling segmentation, relaxing tree assumptions). However, limited data requirements and acknowledged limitations (high dropout, non-end-to-end AC representations, no segmentation) temper expectations of immediate, transformative adoption across the field. | B(4): The paper tackles an important task in argumentation mining—link extraction and AC type classification—by introducing a modified Pointer Network and a joint model that achieves state-of-the-art results on two evaluation corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). As the first neural-network-based approach for this subtask, it is likely to change how researchers model links (moving from feature-rich and ILP methods to end-to-end neural architectures) and to stimulate follow-up work on learning AC representations and segmentation. However, reliance on small training sets and the fact that AC representations are not learned end-to-end (plus segmentation is left unaddressed) may limit immediate widespread adoption, so the impact is strong but not necessarily transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—automatic extraction of argumentative links and component types—and introduces a novel joint pointer-network-based architecture that achieves state-of-the-art results on two corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). As the first neural approach focused on link extraction and AC type classification, it is likely to shift research toward neural, end-to-end methods and inspire follow-up work (e.g., learning AC representations, handling segmentation, relaxing tree assumptions). However, limited data requirements and acknowledged limitations (high dropout, non-end-to-end AC representations, no segmentation) temper expectations of immediate, transformative adoption across the field.

As the first neural-network-based approach for this subtask, it is likely to change how researchers model links (moving from feature-rich and ILP methods to end-to-end neural architectures) and to stimulate follow-up work on learning AC representations and segmentation. However, reliance on small training sets and the fact that AC representations are not learned end-to-end (plus segmentation is left unaddressed) may limit immediate widespread adoption, so the impact is strong but not necessarily transformative.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with the expected flow (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (the modified Pointer Network and the joint AC-type/link prediction model); writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough. Notation and definitions seem adequate (e.g., AC representations, joint loss with hyperparameter α and reported hyperparameters), but the summary notes that evaluation metrics are not specified and the usefulness of figures/tables/examples is not mentioned, suggesting minor presentation gaps. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and presents a clear logical flow from introduction through method and experiments (methodology, datasets, baselines and hyperparameters are all reported) and its main contributions are stated early (joint Pointer Network + AC-type classification). Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, supporting clarity. Some presentation details are missing in the summary (evaluation metrics not specified; figures/tables/examples not mentioned), suggesting minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with the expected flow (introduction → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (the modified Pointer Network and the joint AC-type/link prediction model); writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough. Notation and definitions seem adequate (e.g., AC representations, joint loss with hyperparameter α and reported hyperparameters), but the summary notes that evaluation metrics are not specified and the usefulness of figures/tables/examples is not mentioned, suggesting minor presentation gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized and presents a clear logical flow from introduction through method and experiments (methodology, datasets, baselines and hyperparameters are all reported) and its main contributions are stated early (joint Pointer Network + AC-type classification). Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, supporting clarity. Some presentation details are missing in the summary (evaluation metrics not specified; figures/tables/examples not mentioned), suggesting minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's central claims — a modified Pointer Network and a joint model that predicts AC types and links — are supported by experiments showing state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays and Microtext corpora and by comparisons to reasonable baselines (feature-rich SVM, ILP joint model, MST-based and regular Pointer Network). However, the summary omits key evaluation details (specific metrics and statistical significance), and the approach relies on assumptions and omissions (no end-to-end AC representation learning, no segmentation, and an assumed tree structure) that represent minor methodological gaps though they do not invalidate the reported empirical improvements. | B(4): The main claims (a modified Pointer Network and a joint model for AC type and link prediction) are implemented and evaluated against appropriate baselines on two standard corpora, and the paper reports state-of-the-art results and improvements over a regular Pointer Network. Mathematical content is limited to model architecture (no derivations required) and appears consistent. However, the summary omits details on evaluation metrics and statistical tests, and the model relies on assumptions (partial tree structure) and non–end-to-end AC representations that are acknowledged limitations, which slightly weaken the experimental claims. Overall the technical approach and comparisons are sound but some experimental reporting gaps and assumptions prevent a flawless assessment.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's central claims — a modified Pointer Network and a joint model that predicts AC types and links — are supported by experiments showing state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays and Microtext corpora and by comparisons to reasonable baselines (feature-rich SVM, ILP joint model, MST-based and regular Pointer Network). However, the summary omits key evaluation details (specific metrics and statistical significance), and the approach relies on assumptions and omissions (no end-to-end AC representation learning, no segmentation, and an assumed tree structure) that represent minor methodological gaps though they do not invalidate the reported empirical improvements.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (a modified Pointer Network and a joint model for AC type and link prediction) are implemented and evaluated against appropriate baselines on two standard corpora, and the paper reports state-of-the-art results and improvements over a regular Pointer Network. Mathematical content is limited to model architecture (no derivations required) and appears consistent. However, the summary omits details on evaluation metrics and statistical tests, and the model relies on assumptions (partial tree structure) and non–end-to-end AC representations that are acknowledged limitations, which slightly weaken the experimental claims. Overall the technical approach and comparisons are sound but some experimental reporting gaps and assumptions prevent a flawless assessment.

main_claims: Propose a modified Pointer Network architecture to extract links between argument components (ACs) in argumentative text.

Introduce a joint model that simultaneously predicts AC types (e.g., claim or premise) and links between ACs by combining classification and pointer-based link prediction in a single training objective.
Show that the joint model achieves state-of-the-art results on two evaluation corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts) and outperforms a regular Pointer Network.
Claim this is the first neural network-based approach to argumentation mining (focusing on link extraction and AC type classification).
_summary.methodology: Use a Pointer Network (sequence-to-sequence model with attention) with a bidirectional LSTM encoder and forward LSTM decoder; represent each AC with features, pass representations through fully-connected layers, and add an AC-type classification head with softmax; jointly train link prediction and AC classification with a weighted sum loss (hyperparameter α).
baselines: Base Classifier (Stab and Gurevych, 2016) - feature-rich SVM
Best EG (Peldszus and Stede, 2015) - Evidence Graph
limitations: Requires high dropout due to small amounts of training data, particularly in the Microtext corpus.
AC representations are not learned end-to-end in this work; learning AC representations is left to future work.
Segmentation (subtask 1) is not addressed; a segmenting technique is suggested as future work.
Model assumes a tree structure for linking ACs (partial assumption stated).
